title: Tapping to Anchor in Predictable Calm
subtitle: A daytime tapping practice for building stability as a safe foundation rather than equating it with boredom
excerpt: A five-minute daytime tapping practice from Day 28 of a 30-day sleep programme. The script targets the nervous system pattern of equating calm with danger or boredom — the learned sense that stability is dull or fragile. The reframe moves toward predictable peace as a safe, productive foundation.
sourceNotes: The tapping framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT), in use since the mid-1990s. The script is adapted from the Day 28 daytime tap of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795).

practice.whenToUse: Use when calm or quiet days feel uncomfortable or suspicious rather than welcome — when the nervous system seems to be waiting for something to go wrong even when everything is fine.

content.text: A five-minute daytime tapping practice from Day 28 of a 30-day sleep programme. Day 28 theme: trust rest as stability. The daytime tap works with the nervous system pattern of equating calm with danger or boredom — the learned association between peace and either threat or emptiness. The reframe moves toward stability as the ground from which everything good grows.
New to tapping? Read
how EFT tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Even though my old life ran on chaos, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I used to equate calm with boredom, I honour how far I've come.
Even though stability felt unsafe, I'm open to letting peace be my foundation.
Tapping round
Eyebrow: My nervous system used to crave drama.
Side of eye: I didn't know what to do with quiet.
Under eye: I'm learning that stability isn't dull — it's safe.
Under nose: I can relax without waiting for the next problem.
Chin: I let calm be my new home base.
Collarbone: Predictability gives me freedom.
Under arm: Safety is not stagnation.
Top of head: It's the ground from which I can grow.
Reframe to positive
Eyebrow: I trust peace to hold me steady.
Side of eye: Calm feels like security now.
Under eye: I can build anything from this foundation.
Under nose: My body thrives on stability.
Chin: I'm safe to rest in consistency.
Collarbone: Predictable rhythms support expansion.
Under arm: Peace is my strength, not my weakness.
Top of head: I choose stability as freedom.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from

The tapping framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT), developed in the mid-1990s from Roger Callahan's Thought Field Therapy. The script is adapted from the Day 28 daytime tap of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
